Great Salt Lake Land Cover Change

Comparing LCMS and NLCD land cover products from 1985-2024 over the Great Salt Lake region.

Generated March 13, 2026 at 01:47 PM

Executive Summary

Executive Summary: Great Salt Lake Land Cover Change

This report analyzes land cover fluctuations in the Great Salt Lake region from 1985 to 2024 using data from the Landscape Change Monitoring System (LCMS) and the National Land Cover Database (NLCD). The primary trend identified across both datasets is a substantial reduction in water extent and a corresponding increase in barren surfaces. NLCD data characterizes the study area by a dynamic relationship between open water and barren land, with the former decreasing consistently over the 39-year period.

Transition analysis indicates that while the water class exhibited 53.76% stability between 1985 and 2000, the most frequent land cover conversion throughout the study period was the transition from water to barren or impervious surfaces. The contemporary landscape is dominated by three primary classes: open water, shrub/scrub, and barren land. LCMS data confirms that the loss of water area is the most frequent driver of land cover change in the region, as receding shorelines are replaced by exposed lakebed.

Spatial patterns visible in the 1984 and 1985 baseline maps show a large, contiguous body of water surrounded by a mosaic of shrub/scrub and barren land. The data indicates that land cover transitions are most concentrated along these perimeter interfaces. As the water extent contracted, the resulting land cover shifts occurred primarily at the lake's margins, where former aquatic areas converted to the barren land category.

LCMS Land Cover (1985-2024)

LCMS Land Cover (1985-2024)

The primary land cover trend between 1985 and 2024 is a substantial reduction in Water extent and a corresponding increase in Barren or Impervious surfaces. In 1985, Water accounted for 63.59% of the study area, but this figure declined to 39.46% by 2024, reaching a time-series low of 37.16% in 2022. Simultaneously, Barren or Impervious cover expanded from 8.73% to 30.54%. This inverse relationship represents the most significant compositional shift in the 40-year record, with the most rapid period of change occurring between 2000 and 2003, when Water cover dropped by nearly 10 percentage points.

Vegetation dynamics show a general increase in herbaceous cover and a recent decline in shrubland. Grass/Forb/Herb cover rose from 10.5% in 1985 to 14.08% in 2024, peaking at 15.14% in 2023. In contrast, Shrubs increased through the mid-2000s to a peak of 7.31% in 2007 before declining to 2.54% by 2024. Other classes remained relatively stable or minor: * Trees and tree-related mixes consistently represented less than 2% of the total area. * Grass/Forb/Herb & Shrubs Mix maintained a steady presence, fluctuating between 10% and 12% for the majority of the period.

Spatially, the transition from Water to Barren or Impervious land is concentrated along the margins of the central water body. The animated imagery shows a progressive recession of the shoreline, most notably in the northern and southern reaches of the basin. As the water level dropped, the exposed lakebed transitioned almost immediately to Barren cover. Vegetative expansion, particularly the increase in Grass/Forb/Herb, is observed primarily on the outer edges of these newly exposed areas and within the established terrestrial zones on the western and eastern peripheries.

LCMS Land Cover Transitions

LCMS Land Cover Transitions

Between 1985 and 2000, the landscape exhibited the highest stability within the Water class, which retained 53.76% of the total area. The most frequent transition between different classes was the conversion of Water to Barren or Impervious, accounting for 8.47% of the area. Other notable stable classes during this period included Grass/Forb/Herb & Shrubs Mix (8.49%) and Barren or Impervious (7.14%). Vegetation dynamics were characterized by a shift from Grass/Forb/Herb toward shrub-dominated classes, with 1.88% transitioning to Shrubs and 1.61% to Grass/Forb/Herb & Shrubs Mix.

From 2000 to 2024, the stability of the Water class decreased to 38.65%, while the persistence of Barren or Impervious surfaces increased to 14.1%. The transition from Water to Barren or Impervious remained a primary driver of change, involving 13.56% of the total area. Within vegetated classes, there was a measurable shift toward herbaceous cover: * Grass/Forb/Herb & Shrubs Mix to Grass/Forb/Herb: 2.77% * Shrubs to Grass/Forb/Herb: 1.72% * Shrubs to Grass/Forb/Herb & Shrubs Mix: 1.49%

The long-term data indicates a consistent trend of water loss and the expansion of barren or impervious areas. While classes such as Trees and Shrubs & Trees Mix remained stable but occupied a small percentage of the total area (less than 0.5% each), the Grass/Forb/Herb and Shrubs complexes showed the highest rates of internal flux. By 2024, the Barren or Impervious class represented a significantly larger portion of the landscape than in the 1985–2000 interval, driven by both class persistence and transitions from the Water and Grass/Forb/Herb categories.

NLCD Land Cover (1985-2024)

NLCD Land Cover (1985-2024)

The study area is characterized by a dominant and highly dynamic relationship between Open Water and Barren Land. Between 1985 and 2024, Open Water decreased from a peak of 65.59% (1986) to 41.55% (2024), reaching a historical low of 37.04% in 2022. This decline is directly offset by the expansion of Barren Land, which grew from 5.11% in 1985 to 23.47% in 2024. The spatial data confirms that this transition occurs primarily along the receding margins of the central water body, indicating a significant and persistent contraction of the shoreline over the 40-year period.

Vegetative cover and anthropogenic development show more gradual but steady trends: * Shrub/Scrub remains the primary terrestrial land cover, increasing from 17.87% to 20.78%. * Grassland/Herbaceous and Cultivated Crops both experienced net declines, ending the period at 7.47% and 1.20%, respectively. * Developed classes (Open Space through High Intensity) represent a small portion of the total area but showed consistent growth, nearly doubling from an aggregate of 0.64% in 1985 to 1.05% in 2024. * Emergent Herbaceous Wetlands exhibited high inter-annual variability, fluctuating between 1.46% and 4.05% in response to changing water levels.

Spatially, the most significant changes are concentrated in the basin interior, where the central water body has fragmented and retreated, leaving behind expansive rings of Barren Land. Developed areas are concentrated in the northeastern and southwestern peripheries, showing incremental outward expansion into adjacent Grassland and Shrub/Scrub zones. The Shrub/Scrub class maintains a stable presence in the higher-elevation upland areas, while the wetland and barren classes remain highly sensitive to the fluctuating perimeter of the Open Water.

NLCD Land Cover Transitions

NLCD Land Cover Transitions

Analysis of land cover transitions between 1985, 2000, and 2024 reveals a landscape dominated by Open Water, Shrub/Scrub, and Barren Land, with significant flux occurring between these categories. From 1985 to 2000, the most substantial stable class was Open Water, with 55.54% of the total area remaining unchanged. During this same period, 15.89% of the landscape persisted as Shrub/Scrub. The most notable transition involved the conversion of 5.63% of 1985 Open Water into Barren Land by 2000.

The transition period from 2000 to 2024 indicates an acceleration in the conversion of aquatic features to terrestrial classes. The area of persistent Open Water decreased to 40.7%, while the transition from Open Water to Barren Land increased significantly to 13.94%. Simultaneously, Shrub/Scrub showed high retention at 16.57%, supplemented by a 3.36% transition from Grassland/Herbaceous areas. Emergent Herbaceous Wetlands also saw a notable shift, with 3.0% of the 2000 extent persisting through 2024, while 0.73% of former Open Water transitioned into this category.

Developed and agricultural classes represent a small but stable fraction of the total land cover. Developed, Low Intensity areas maintained a consistent footprint, with 0.29% persistence between 1985–2000 and 0.31% between 2000–2024. Cultivated Crops and Pasture/Hay exhibited minimal change, with the majority of these classes remaining stable or undergoing minor internal exchanges with Grassland/Herbaceous and Shrub/Scrub categories. Overall, the data reflects a trend of receding water levels and the subsequent expansion of barren and shrub-dominated landscapes.
